definition: Service Level Objective. A target value for a service reliability metric, such as availability or latency. SLOs define the expected level of service and form the basis of error budgets.
Service Level Indicator. A quantitative measure of a service attribute, such as request success rate, latency percentile, or throughput. SLIs are the metrics that SLOs are defined against.

The amount of unreliability permitted by an SLO. Calculated as 1 minus the SLO target. A 99.9% availability SLO gives 43.2 minutes of downtime budget per month. Consumed by outages, deployments, and experiments.
Manual, repetitive, automatable operational work that scales linearly with service growth and provides no enduring value. SRE teams aim to keep toil below 50% of working time.
A blameless written record of an incident that documents the timeline, root cause, impact, and action items. The goal is learning and prevention, not assigning blame.
A reliability pattern that prevents cascading failures by stopping requests to a failing service. Has three states: Closed (normal), Open (failing fast), and Half-Open (probing recovery).
The four key metrics for monitoring a service: Latency (how long requests take), Traffic (how much demand), Errors (rate of failed requests), and Saturation (how full the service is).

The Linux kernel mechanism that terminates processes when the system or a cgroup runs out of memory. Sends SIGKILL to the process with the highest badness score. In Kubernetes, a container exceeding its memory limit triggers the cgroup-scoped OOM killer.

Completely Fair Scheduler. The Linux default process scheduler that uses virtual runtime to give each process a proportional share of CPU time. CPU limits in Kubernetes are enforced via CFS bandwidth throttling.
A Kubernetes policy that limits voluntary disruptions to a set of pods. Ensures a minimum number of pods remain available during node drains, cluster autoscaler events, and rolling updates.
A Kubernetes controller that automatically scales the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU utilization or custom metrics. Uses a proportional control loop running every 15 seconds.
A distributed key-value store built on the Raft consensus algorithm. The single source of truth for all Kubernetes cluster state. Only kube-apiserver communicates with etcd directly.
A Kubernetes health check that determines if a container is ready to receive traffic. Failure removes the pod IP from Service endpoints. Does not restart the container, unlike a liveness probe.
A performance analysis methodology by Brendan Gregg. For every resource, check Utilization, Saturation, and Errors. Systematically identifies bottlenecks in CPU, memory, disk, and network.
Mutual TLS. An extension of TLS where both client and server present certificates and verify each other's identity. Used in service meshes like Istio for zero-trust service-to-service authentication.
